title: ROGOLF

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify the game as a minigolf roguelite: each run is different, levels have their own rules, players spend shots as score, collect coins for multipliers, buy equipment/power-ups, and progress through gates toward higher stages. This is strong evidence for a run-based score-chasing sports/arcade structure with roguelite modifiers. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a fitting minigolf/golf-arcade/roguelite sports archetype. 'sports_sim' is a poor fit because the game is not presented as simulation-focused, and other archetypes are even less appropriate. Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's two main axes reasonably well: golf/minigolf course play and run-based roguelite/upgrade structure. The best candidate combines both directly, while the rest each cover only one side strongly.
anchor_summary: ROGOLF is a run-based arcade minigolf roguelite: shot-limited holes, coin/multiplier scoring, equipment and power-up purchasing, rule-changing stages, and progression through gates toward higher floors.
gap_notes: The rail has only one clearly direct golf-roguelite comparison; most others split between minigolf without roguelite structure or roguelites with non-golf shot mechanics.
Multiplayer/party minigolf candidates are somewhat overrepresented relative to ROGOLF's run-based progression identity.
A stronger rail would prioritize more single-player arcade sports or physics-shot roguelites if available.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The clearest fit: golf-first play, every shot as a scarce resource, hazard-heavy hole progression, power-ups, replayability, and explicit roguelite structure align very closely with ROGOLF.
Very close on arcade minigolf, gimmick courses, score/challenge focus, and power-ups. It lacks the anchor's run-based roguelite economy, but the core on-course play is highly relevant.
Strong match on run-based progression, shops/relics, and physics-based aiming, but its combat-driven pachinko loop is materially different from minigolf hole completion and score-gating.
Shares physics-shot play, randomized runs, loot, and roguelite progression, but it is fundamentally a dungeon crawler with character classes and enemy combat rather than a golf game.

Understandable because it is obstacle-heavy minigolf with power-ups, but its party/multiplayer focus and mode structure are a notable mismatch for ROGOLF's single-run upgrade-driven roguelite identity.

title: Resident Evil 2 (1998)

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store/meta descriptions consistently frame the game as a horror-focused survival experience: 'ultimate test of survival,' 'face your fears,' 'terror-filled classic,' with relentless zombies and mutant creatures. Metacritic explicitly tags the genre as Survival, and the copy describes two authored character campaigns centered on escaping a biotech outbreak in Raccoon City. The presentation is described as cutting-edge 3-D/photorealistic for its era, supporting third-person 3D survival-horror identity. Evidence is strong for survival_horror at the archetype level, though several lower-level mechanical facets like exact topology and combat structure are not explicitly described in the stored text.

overall_note: The set has two very strong classic Resident Evil comps, one reasonable but more modernized franchise neighbor, and one broader survival-horror pick that fits on genre structure more than close gameplay identity.
anchor_summary: Resident Evil 2 (1998) is classic fixed-camera survival horror: single-player exploration through interconnected spaces, puzzle-gated progression, scarce resources, deliberate combat, and high tension built around route planning and survival pressure.
gap_notes: The rail is strongest when it stays with classic fixed-camera / pre-rendered-era survival horror structure.
A modern over-the-shoulder remake is meaningfully less identity-close despite shared story/franchise context.
Silent Hill is an understandable genre neighbor, but it is not as direct a mechanical match as classic Resident Evil entries.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-direct match in classic Resident Evil survival-horror design: fixed-camera era pacing, resource management, puzzle-led routing, authored single-player campaign, and very similar combat/exploration tension.
The foundational template for RE2’s gameplay identity: fixed-camera survival horror, limited ammo, puzzles, backtracking, and tightly authored single-player progression.
Still survival-horror with escape pressure and item management, but the modern over-the-shoulder presentation, more action-forward flow, and included multiplayer make it a looser gameplay match.

Shares horror exploration, puzzle-solving, and constrained survival framing, but it leans toward psychological-horror identity and is not as specifically aligned with RE2’s classic combat/exploration structure.

title: I Am Jesus Christ

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies the game as a first-person narrative experience about reliving the life of Jesus, exploring Holy Land locations, meeting biblical figures, resisting temptation, reading scripture, praying, and performing miracles. However, the available copy does not establish a clear gameplay structure that maps strongly to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. There is no strong evidence for shooter, stealth, survival, RPG, immersive sim, puzzle, platforming, management, tactics, or sports/racing identity. Combat is unclear, progression is unclear, and the phrase 'simulation' appears only as a storefront genre label rather than a described systems loop. This looks closer to a first-person narrative/adventure or walking-sim style experience, which is not represented in the allowed archetype list, so classification would be too speculative.

overall_note: The rail is directionally right: all candidates target the anchor's likely gameplay identity as a low-challenge, first-person narrative exploration experience. Best fits are authored walking-sim/story games with guided progression; the looser or more formally experimental entries fit less cleanly.

gap_notes: No candidate mirrors the anchor's explicit miracle-performing and biblical reenactment structure, so the set captures format better than specific interaction verbs.

Given the anchor's unclear exact structure, this set is sensible, but it leans heavily on the walking-sim side of the spectrum.

title: Living Dead House

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports an arcade-style undead survival action game with solo/local co-op, leaderboard competition, 20 stages, weapons/items, and Survival/Arcade modes. However, the evidence does not clearly establish a supported taxonomy archetype: perspective is unspecified, moment-to-moment structure is too vague to distinguish between beat_em_up, action_horror, co_op_horror, or another action subtype, and the current computed near-misses (party_game, sports_sim) are clearly false positives from sparse metadata. Because the core gameplay identity cannot be mapped confidently to one of the allowed archetypes, rejection is safer than forcing a weak fit.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally solid: all candidates are arcade-action shooters with meaningful overlap in co-op, replayable combat, and score/survival structure. The best fits are top-down arena shooters with strong leaderboard and survival DNA. Lower-ranked entries drift toward campaign progression or classic run-based rescue structure rather than the anchor’s likely tighter wave-survival arcade loop.
anchor_summary: Living Dead House appears to be a compact retro arcade action shooter centered on surviving waves of undead across short stages or survival modes, playable solo or in local co-op, with leaderboard-driven replayability and light weapon/item variety. The strongest comps should therefore be arcade-first twin-stick/arena shooters with stage-based or wave-based survival structure, local co-op, and score-chasing rather than broader campaign shooters or games that only share zombie theming.
gap_notes: The set does a good job targeting arcade shooters rather than over-indexing on zombie theme alone.

title: Fishbowl

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions repeatedly and explicitly call Fishbowl a 'visual novel' and a narrative slice-of-life coming-of-age story. The core loop centers on making choices over the course of a month, video-calling friends/family/coworkers, doing daily care/work tasks, and sorting through belongings and memory-related puzzles. The text stresses 'no bad endings, just different paths' and says 'the choices you make will shape the possibilities in Alo's future,' which strongly supports authored branching and dialogue/relationship-focused interaction. No combat is described anywhere; instead the gameplay is framed as intimate, cozy, grief-focused narrative progression with light puzzle/matching activities. That combination fits visual_novel much better than broader adventure labels.

overall_note: The set is directionally strong for narrative-first, low-challenge, emotionally intimate games, with one especially close visual-novel comp. The main gap is that several picks drift toward adventure exploration or highly linear interactive art pieces rather than Fishbowl’s choice-shaped slice-of-life visual novel structure.

title: Clean Up Earth

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a relaxing environmental restoration/cleanup game focused on vacuuming waste, recycling materials, rebuilding damaged structures, exploring large maps, and cooperating with up to 25 players. The strongest supported traits are multiplayer/co-op support, large shared spaces, casual/simulation framing, and non-combat restorative play. However, the Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a suitable home for a cooperative cleanup/restoration exploration sim with minimal or no combat. The closest available archetypes are poor fits because they require identity-defining elements not supported here, such as farming/life loops, factory/engineering systems, colony/city management, survival pressures, shooter combat, or RPG progression. Because the gameplay identity is clear but falls outside the available archetype set, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate category.

overall_note: The rail has a strong top end because several candidates match the anchor's cleanup-and-restoration core loop closely. The main gap is that most candidates are single-player and smaller in structure, so the anchor's co-op/community-play identity is underrepresented.

title: Darwin's Paradox!

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently frame Darwin's Paradox! as a single-player, story-driven 3D platformer-adventure with puzzle solving and light stealth. The strongest recurring gameplay signals are 'platforming sequences,' 'moments of puzzle-solving,' and verbs like swim, wall-climb, hide, infiltrate, jump, camouflage, and shoot ink to overcome traps and escape. The presentation is described as a cinematic animated-movie-like adventure in a mysterious industrial complex with sprawling handcrafted levels, which supports a linear/level-based campaign structure and a secondary cinematic action-adventure flavor. Combat is not presented as dominant; instead, stealthy avoidance, traversal, and puzzle progression appear to define the core loop, making action_platformer the best fit.

title: Enter the chronosphere

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a single-player, procedurally generated run-based top-down shooter/roguelike with a distinctive hybrid structure: turn-based tactical planning blended with real-time bullet-hell action, plus build synergies, unlockable characters, weapons, and missions. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a good-fit archetype for a single-player tactical/bullet-hell roguelike shooter of this kind. Available shooter archetypes such as arena_fps, military_fps, tactical_shooter, hero_shooter, loot_shooter, or extraction_shooter do not match the top-down run-based roguelike identity, and turn_based_tactics would also be misleading because the game is explicitly a shooter with hybrid real-time bullet-hell combat rather than primarily overhead squad tactics. Because the gameplay evidence is strong but does not map cleanly to an allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

overall_note: The candidate set captures the anchor's unusual hybrid identity from multiple angles: movement-linked time control, top-down run-based shooting, and roguelike progression. The best comps are games that share the anchor's turn-based-per-move combat logic and ranged roguelike structure; several others are solid but looser because they match only the bullet-hell shooter side or only the movement-timing side.
anchor_summary: Enter the Chronosphere is a single-player top-down roguelike shooter built around a distinctive hybrid combat loop: time advances only when you move, creating a turn-based tactical layer inside bullet-hell projectile dodging. Core identity is procedural runs, ranged combat, build synergies, unlockable characters/equipment, and high-pressure spatial planning.
gap_notes: The rail has no near-exact comp for the anchor's combined identity of top-down shooter + roguelike runs + 'time moves when you move' bullet management.
Several candidates split the identity into separate halves: SUPERHOT matches the temporal combat rule, while Enter the Gungeon and SYNTHETIK match the run-based top-down shooter side.
One candidate leans too far into rhythm/melee timing rather than shooter combat, making it a weaker recommendation despite movement-linked enemy turns.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest overall match: single-player sci-fi roguelike with top-down ranged combat, procedural runs, build/perk progression, and enemies acting when you act. Less bullet-hell and less real-time pressure, but the gameplay identity is extremely close.

Shares the anchor's signature 'time moves only when you move' combat logic and projectile-dodging/shooting cadence. Much looser on perspective, run structure, and progression, but still a very strong mechanic-level comp.

Very strong match on top-down bullet-hell shooting, room/floor-based roguelite runs, weapon variety, dodging, and high-pressure combat. Missing the anchor's move-linked temporal tactics, but highly compatible on the shooter-roguelike side.

Good fit for top-down run-based shooting, randomized loot/perks, build shaping, and harsh roguelite challenge. Weaker because it is fully real-time and emphasizes gunfeel/action intensity more than temporal tactics.

Understandable comparison because movement timing governs enemy interaction in a run-based top-down roguelike. But it is fundamentally a rhythm/melee game rather than a shooter or bullet-hell tactics hybrid, so the fit is stretched.

title: Super Meat Boy 3D

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly define the game as a tough-as-nails 3D platformer centered on 'straight forward twitch reflex platforming' and 'precision platforming action.' The text emphasizes brutal but fair levels, repeated deaths from hazards, wall-leaping, and escalating difficulty, which strongly matches precision_platformer. Metacritic genre labeling supports third-person 3D platforming, while the copy's references to 'a bunch of levels,' Dark World levels, and boss fights support a level-based campaign structure with boss-centric encounters. Combat is not described as a primary loop, so the game fits precision platformer far better than action_platformer or 3d_collectathon; unlockable secrets are present, but collection is not framed as the core objective.

overall_note: The rail has a very strong top end anchored by the obvious Super Meat Boy and Celeste comps, plus a few reasonable 3D neighbors. The main gap is that only some candidates preserve the anchor's pure precision-platforming identity; others drift toward exploration, collectibles, combat, or broader mascot-platformer structure.
anchor_summary: Super Meat Boy 3D is a single-player, level-based third-person precision platformer built around brutal-but-fair hazard courses, instant-retry mastery, tight movement, escalating difficulty, secrets, and some boss fights. Its identity is much closer to pure repeated-death precision platforming than to exploration-heavy 3D platformers or combat-forward action platformers.
gap_notes: The best fit is the original Super Meat Boy despite the 2D/3D shift; lineage and core loop alignment outweigh perspective mismatch.
Celeste is an excellent precision-platforming neighbor, but also 2D and more narrative-driven than the anchor.

Blue Fire and Super Sami Roll help cover the anchor's 3D movement angle, but both are looser fits because one is combat/exploration-heavy and the other leans more toward rolling/collectathon challenge design.
Crash Bandicoot 4 is a valid 3D level-based platforming comp, but its crate-collecting, character-swapping, and broader action-platformer identity make it less precision-pure than the anchor.
A stronger rail would ideally include more 3D games centered specifically on short, hazard-dense precision stages with rapid retry and minimal combat.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct predecessor and clearest benchmark: same instant-retry, hazard-dense, mastery-heavy precision platforming structure, only in 2D rather than 3D.
Extremely close on repeated-death precision platforming, handcrafted challenge rooms, tight movement, and fast respawns, though it is 2D and more narrative-focused.
A meaningful 3D platforming comp with short stages, movement mastery, and escalating challenge, but rolling physics, secrets, and broader platformer framing make it less directly Meat Boy-like.
Strong 3D movement challenge overlap, especially in trap gauntlets and precision trials, but combat, exploration, quests, upgrades, and metroidvania progression are much more central.
Shares level-based 3D platforming, replayable stages, and boss encounters, but its broader mascot action-platformer identity and collectible focus make it a looser precision-platforming match.

title: Legacy of Kain: Ascendance

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify the game as a 'fast, 2D action platformer' centered on vertical movement, fluid combat, and skill-driven play. Multiple sources reinforce side-scrolling/2D platforming, while the Steam detailed description adds concrete combat signals: chaining melee strikes, evasive dashes, supernatural attacks, and level-based environmental challenges/puzzles flowing into combat. The copy also supports a linear campaign-like single-player structure, dark fantasy setting, pixel art presentation, and high-execution platforming/action focus. Evidence is strong and internally consistent for action_platformer rather than metroidvania or a pure platformer.

overall_note: The candidate set mostly targets the right space: stage-structured 2D action-platformers with meaningful combat-platforming interplay. Best fits are games that stay level-based and combat-forward; candidates that lean more into metroidvania exploration or tonal/comedic detours are weaker for this anchor.
anchor_summary: Legacy of Kain: Ascendance appears to be a single-player, level-based 2D action-platformer focused on fast traversal, fluid melee-led combat, environmental hazards/puzzles, boss encounters, and skill-driven execution, with some emphasis on multiple playable characters and vertical movement.
gap_notes: The rail is strongest on general 2D combat-platforming, but only some candidates reflect the anchor's stated multiple-protagonist angle.
A few candidates drift toward metroidvania or exploration-heavy progression, which is a weaker match for an explicitly level-based anchor.
No candidate is a perfect benchmark for the anchor's specific mix of vertical movement, fluid melee combat, and character variety, though several are close neighbors.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit on level-based 2D action-platforming, dark combat focus, boss-driven stages, and multiple playable characters with distinct abilities.
Excellent match for fast stage-based 2D hack-and-slash platforming with traps, bosses, evasive mobility, and a gothic supernatural combat loop.
Strong overlap in brutal, skill-heavy 2D action-platforming and linear combat progression, though its gun-heavy arsenal and sci-fi flavor make it less identity-aligned.
Good combat-platforming and character-swapping comp, but boss-unlocked routing and castle exploration push it noticeably toward metroidvania structure.
Shares tight side-scrolling combat and acrobatic traversal, but its later exploratory structure and lighter, more comedic identity are looser fits for the anchor.

title: Hozy

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Hozy as a cozy restoration/decorating simulation focused on cleaning, painting, furnishing, and interacting with objects across nine restoreable locations, with no timers, scores, penalties, or combat. The evidence is strong for a non-combat cozy renovation sim loop, but the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype set does not provide a clear fit for this gameplay identity. It is not well-supported as life_sim, management_tycoon, hidden_object_puzzle, or any action/adventure/RPG/shooter/racing archetype. Because the gameplay is clear yet falls outside the available archetypes, this should be rejected rather than forced into a poor match.

overall_note: The proposed rail is pointed in the right direction: it captures first-person renovation, cleanup, and decorating sims well. The strongest fits are the House Flipper games, while the rest are progressively looser neighbors that each drop one important part of Hozy's identity, especially the calm home-decoration focus and lack of management pressure.
anchor_summary: Hozy is a cozy first-person restoration sim built around cleaning, painting, repairing, decorating, and arranging lived-in spaces at a no-pressure pace, with no combat, no timers, and satisfaction coming from tactile tool use plus visual transformation of rooms.
gap_notes: The rail has the right center of gravity around renovation sims, but only the House Flipper titles feel truly same-audience obvious.
Some candidates drift toward business/management framing, which is weaker for Hozy than pure restore-and-decor flow.
PowerWash Simulator matches the soothing tactile cleanup side very well, but misses the furnishing/interior design half of the loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Extremely close fit: first-person cleaning, painting, repairing, and furnishing spaces with a relaxed creative loop. Sandbox/build options broaden it, but the core restore-and-decor play is a near match.

Another obvious benchmark for Hozy: tool-based cleanup, wall work, installation, and interior decoration drive the experience. It is a bit more money/profit-oriented, but the moment-to-moment gameplay is very close.

Good fit on first-person teardown, renovation, and furnishing, but it leans more into hotel management/business progression than Hozy's low-pressure room-by-room restoration focus.

Very good match for soothing, tactile, no-pressure cleaning, but it lacks Hozy's painting, furnishing, and interior design/restoration loop, so it lands as a narrower neighboring fantasy rather than a close equivalent.

title: Aether & Iron

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam copy gives clear gameplay identity: a single-player narrative RPG where you recruit companions, level abilities, make consequential choices, and engage in 'tactical turn-based combat' with an 'interesting twist on strategic, turn-based vehicular battles.' The text repeatedly emphasizes crew management, tactical encounters, mystery-solving in an alternate 1930s floating New York, and choice-driven story progression. That supports turn_based_tactics as the core play identity, with western_narrative_rpg as a secondary layer.

overall_note: The rail has several strong party-based tactical narrative RPG comps, especially the Shadowrun titles and Wasteland 3. It weakens toward the bottom where narrative/investigation overlap is present but the core tactical party-combat identity is much looser.
anchor_summary: Aether & Iron is a single-player, authored narrative RPG built around recruited companions, character progression, choice-and-skill-check storytelling, mystery/investigation framing, and tactical turn-based combat with a notable vehicle/crew-management twist. Best matches should preserve the blend of party tactics and story-led RPG structure, not just alt-history or pulp vibes.
gap_notes: Top of rail is solid for story-led squad tactics, but none of the candidates fully mirror the anchor's vehicular tactical combat twist.
Sovereign Syndicate shares investigation and narrative-RPG structure, but lacks the anchor's party-tactics core and should sit below the tactical RPG comps.
The Lamplighters League is a good fit, though its real-time infiltration layer makes it slightly less direct than the pure turn-based squad RPGs.
